coredata.dc:title: Mesopore-dominated hollow carbon nanoparticles prepared by simple air oxidation of carbon black for high mass loading supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Chengwei
Dong, Yue
Liu, Yifan
Zhang, Lihui
Wang, Dengke
Lin, Xieji
Lv, Yan
Zhang, Su
Song, Huaihe
Jia, Dianzeng
coredata.dc:description: With the ever-increasing demands for miniaturization of high-power supercapacitors, carbon materials maintaining good rate capability at high mass loadings are urgently needed. Herein, we prepare mesopore-dominated hollow carbon nanoparticles with good capacitive performance from commercial carbon black through an extremely simple air oxidation method. Taking advantage of the crystallinity inhomogeneity of carbon black nanoparticles, the hollow carbon nanoparticles are prepared by selective removal of the amorphous cores while maintaining of the ordered skins. The hollow carbon nanoparticles show large inner cavities, perforated mesoporous structure, and increased surface area from 87.4 to 753.2 m2 g−1, providing abundant electrolyte reservoirs, ion migration channels, and active sites especially for thick electrodes. The sample shows a dramatically enhanced capacitance from 5 F g−1 of the carbon black to 175 F g−1. Importantly, the assembled symmetric device exhibits high areal capacitance of 2.31 F cm−2 at 1 A g−1 and remarkable rate performance of 1.59 F cm−2 at 10 A g−1 even with an ultrahigh mass loading of 15 mg cm−2. Our work helps the deep understanding of the structural transformation of carbon black, and provides a scalable preparation route for hollow carbon nanoparticles with great potential for supercapacitors application.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TGA-DSC curve of N330 nanoparticles in air atmosphere. (b) The carbon residues at different oxidation times. HRTEM images of (c1, c2) N330 and (d1, d2) N330-5h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) N2 adsorption desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distributions of the N330 and N330-5h. (c) Variation of specific surface area and pore volume of the samples. (d) Scheme of the structural transformation process during air oxidation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XRD patterns and (b) ratio of intensity and FWHM of the (002) peak of N330-x. (c) Raman spectra of N330 and N330-5h. (d) Variation of ID/IG of N330-x. (e) XPS survey spectra and (f) C1s spectra of N330 and N330-5h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Electric conductivity of N330-x and commercial activated carbon YP-50. The electrochemical performance measured by three-electrode system: (b) CV curves at 100 mV s−1, (c) galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles at 1 A g−1, (d) rate performance, (e) Nyquist plots, and (f) Bode plots of N330-x. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The electrochemical performance of N330-5h//N330-5h symmetric supercapacitors at different mass loadings: (a) CV curves at 100 mV s−1, (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles at 1 A g−1, (c) areal capacitances at 1 and 10 A g−1. (d) Nyquist plots at different mass loadings. (e) Scheme of the good capacitive performance of N330-5h thick electrode. (f) Cycle performance of N330-5h//N330-5h supercapacitor at 10 A g−1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Mesopore-dominated hollow carbon nanoparticles prepared by simple air oxidation of carbon black for high mass loading supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830046, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resources Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resources Engineering
Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resources Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the ever-increasing demands for miniaturization of high-power supercapacitors, carbon materials maintaining good rate capability at high mass loadings are urgently needed. Herein, we prepare mesopore-dominated hollow carbon nanoparticles with good capacitive performance from commercial carbon black through an extremely simple air oxidation method. Taking advantage of the crystallinity inhomogeneity of carbon black nanoparticles, the hollow carbon nanoparticles are prepared by selective removal of the amorphous cores while maintaining of the ordered skins. The hollow carbon nanoparticles show large inner cavities, perforated mesoporous structure, and increased surface area from 87.4 to 753.2 m2 g−1, providing abundant electrolyte reservoirs, ion migration channels, and active sites especially for thick electrodes. The sample shows a dramatically enhanced capacitance from 5 F g−1 of the carbon black to 175 F g−1. Importantly, the assembled symmetric device exhibits high areal capacitance of 2.31 F cm−2 at 1 A g−1 and remarkable rate performance of 1.59 F cm−2 at 10 A g−1 even with an ultrahigh mass loading of 15 mg cm−2. Our work helps the deep understanding of the structural transformation of carbon black, and provides a scalable preparation route for hollow carbon nanoparticles with great potential for supercapacitors application.

Mesopore-dominated hollow carbon nanoparticles prepared by air oxidation of carbon black nanoparticles show high capacitive and fast electrochemical kinetics at high mass loadings due to abundant electrolyte reservoirs, perforated porous structure, and high surface area.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have been widely explored in fundamental research and industrial manufacture because of their high power performance, long lifespan, and low maintenance cost [1–4]. Carbon materials with various morphologies and properties have been developed to meet the high capacitance and fast energy storage requirements of supercapacitors [5–9]. Along with the miniaturization devices with high capacitance and power density, exploring electrodes with high active mass loadings become an important direction for future development. For example, commercial available supercapacitors require a mass loading of more than 10 mg cm−2 on single electrode [10]. Most carbon materials show unsatisfied capacitance and rate performance in this condition because of the severely blocked ion migration channels as well as the insufficient material utilization in bulk phase. To overcome this problem, constructing interconnected ion migration channels and electrolyte reservoirs in thick electrodes is of great importance.

Carbon black is a kind of traditional material which has been widely used as filler in rubbers, conductive additive in electrode materials, and catalyst supports due to its low cost, high electric conductivity, and small size [11–15]. However, it is barely applied as the electrode material for supercapacitors due to the limited capacitance as a result of its low specific surface area. Carbon black is prepared by pyrolysis of carbon-rich precursors [16]. The formation process can be divided into nucleation and growth stage, giving the carbon black nanoparticles a unique core-skin structure with an amorphous core formed by loosely stacked carbon flakes and a relatively well-ordered outer skin [17–20]. Due to the crystallinity inhomogeneity, a few studies show that the amorphous cores with high reactivity can be selectively etched while the ordered skins are maintained through thermal or nitric acid oxidation, forming a hollow structure [15,21,22]. Early in 1950s, Polley et al. found that the oxidation at 600 °C of carbon black increased its surface area about sixfold without diameter change, attributable to the development of porosity [23]. Similar phenomena are also reported in some literatures [24–27]. But insight of the structural transformation and the electrochemical properties of the obtained hollow carbon nanoparticles (HCNPs) have not been deeply investigated.

Herein, we prepared mesopore-dominated HCNPs from solid carbon black nanoparticles through an extremely simple air oxidation method. During controllable oxidation, mesopores (3 nm) are gradually formed and perforate through the skins of carbon black nanoparticles. The amorphous cores with high reactivity are subsequently etched away, and inner cavities (18 nm) are eventually formed. Therefore, the HCNPs show a mesopore-dominated hollow structure with increased surface area from 87.4 to 753.2 m2 g−1. The inner cavities act as electrolyte reservoirs in thick electrodes, and the interconnected mesopores greatly shorten the ion migration distance, enabling high rate capability especially at high mass loadings. Moreover, the introduced oxygen species can improve the wettability of HCNPs toward aqueous electrolyte and provide additional pseudocapacitance. Therefore, the assembled symmetric device with an ultrahigh mass loading of 15 mg cm−2 shows areal capacitances of 2.31 and 1.59 F cm−2 at 1 and 10 A g−1, respectively, indicating the practical application value of the as-prepared HCNPs.

A commercial N330 carbon black was chosen as the precursor of HCNPs. Typically, 2 g N330 carbon black was oxidized at 450 °C for 1, 3, 5, and 7 h in air atmosphere in a muffle oven. The samples are denoted as “N330-x”, where x stands for the air oxidation time.

Thermogravimetric analysis and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (TGA-DSC) was performed on a NETZSCH STA 449C analyzer with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from room temperature to 1000 °C in air atmosphere. The hollow structure of samples was observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F). The surface area and pore volume of samples were measured by N2 adsorption desorption isotherms (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics) based on the Braunauer-Emmett-Teller method. And the pore size distribution was obtained by density functional theory method. The structure and composition of samples were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance), Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Xi), X-ray Auger electron spectroscopy (XAES, ESCALAB 250 Xi), and contact angle measurement (KRUSS DSA 100).

For the preparation of working electrodes, 85 wt% of the sample, 10 wt% of conductive carbon black, and 5 wt% of polytetrafluoroethylene were mixed using ethanol as solvent. Then the slurry was coated onto nickel foam (1 × 1 cm2) as the current collector to obtain the working electrode. The electrodes were tested by three- and two-electrode systems in 6 mol L−1 KOH aqueous electrolyte according to our previous works [28–30]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge measurements were recorded on a electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua Co. Ltd., China). The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were performed in the frequency range from 0.01 to 100,000 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV.

Firstly, TGA-DSC is used to investigate the oxidation behavior of the N330 nanoparticles. As shown in the TGA curve (Fig. 1 a), the air oxidation begins at 450 °C. After a rapid mass loss at ca. 550 °C, it terminates at 600 °C with a mass loss of 97%. The corresponding DSC curve shows two clearly identified exothermic peaks at 550.7 and 582.0 °C, indicating the different oxidation stability of amorphous cores and ordered skins of the N330 nanoparticles. The low-temperature peak is ascribed to the removal of amorphous cores because of their high reactivity. Moreover, the intensity difference between these two peaks indicates that oxidation of the amorphous cores is the dominant process. According to the TGA-DSC results, we adopt 450 °C as the oxidation temperature in our work to realize controllable preparation of HCNPs with different morphologies and structures. Through oxidation at 450 °C, the disordered cores are gradually etched and removed, leading to the decreased carbon residue along with the oxidation time (Fig. 1b).

From the HRTEM images, N330 nanoparticles exhibit a solid spherical shape with an average diameter of 40 nm (Fig. 1c1). The core-skin structure with amorphous cores and ordered skins is clearly observed in Fig. 1c2 [16,31]. The N330 nanoparticles are completely transformed from solid to hollow particles after oxidation for 5 h, as observed from the HRTEM images of N330-5h (Fig. 1d1 and d2). It is worth mentioned that the nanoparticle size is almost unchanged after oxidation. But the skin regions of N330-5h become more disordered than those of the N330 precursor, which is ascribed to the distortion of crystal lattices caused by the introduced pores and oxygen functional groups.

The nitrogen adsorption desorption isotherms of N330 and N330-5h (Fig. 2 a, Fig. S1) exhibit type IV features consisting of a rapid adsorption at low relative pressure region (<0.1), an adsorption plateau at medium relative pressure region from 0.1 to 0.9, and a hysteresis loop at high relative pressure region (>0.9), meaning the primary mesoporous structure [28]. From the size distribution in Fig. 2b, the original N330 is nearly nonporous with small amount of pores concentrated at 40–50 nm, which come from the voids in between the N330 nanoparticles. Small amount of mesopores (3.0 nm) and some micropores are observed in N330-5h due to the etching of carbon by the oxygen. These micro- and meso-pores perforate the skins of N330 nanoparticles, further leading to the etching of the amorphous cores and the formation of inner cavities (18 nm). The specific surface area increases from 87.4 m2 g−1 for N330 to 753.2 m2 g−1 for N330-5h (Fig. 2c), which is beneficial for the capacitive energy storage. Importantly, the mesopores volume monotonically increases from N330-1h to N330-7h, demonstrating the gradual evolution of mesopore-dominated structure of HCNPs. Accordingly, the scheme of structural transformation is shown in Fig. 2d. The oxygen first attack the reactive sites on the outer surface of N330 nanoparticles during oxidation, forming perforated mesopores on the skins. Thereafter, the oxygen penetrates the skins and etches the amorphous cores. The gaseous products such as carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide are released through the perforated mesopores, leading to the formation of large inner cavities and interconnected mesoporous structure.

The XRD patterns of all the samples are shown in Fig. 3 a. The peaks at ca. 25° and 43° correspond to the (002) and (100) diffraction peaks of carbon, respectively. The crystal degree can be estimated by the ratio of intensity and full width at half maximum (FWHM) of (002) peak (Fig. 3b). The ratio slightly increases from 241.7 for N330 to 263.4 for N330-1h. The initial crystallinity enhancement is due to the removal of inevitably presented amorphous carbon on the surface of N330 nanoparticles. Subsequently, the crystallinity monotonically decreases due to the introduction of pores and oxygen-bearing groups by prolonged oxidation time, as confirmed by the HRTEM images.

The Raman spectra are fitted into four peaks of T- (1200 cm−1), D- (1340 cm−1), D’- (1480 cm−1), and G-band (1580 cm−1) (Fig. 3c, Fig. S2) [7,32]. The area ratio of D- and G-band (ID/IG) first decreases from 0.98 for N330 to 0.94 for N330-1h and then increases afterwards (Fig. 3d). The defect density change is in accordance with the XRD patterns. It should be noted that the T- and D′-band are obviously strengthened and broadened in N330-5h, and the G-band blue-shifts from 1571 cm−1 for N330 to 1582 cm−1 for N330-5h. These phenomena are due to the introduction of negatively charged oxygen species (see the XPS results below) [33–35].

The chemical composition of the samples is investigated by XPS. The oxygen content dramatically increases from 6.2 at.% for N330 to 16.2 at.% for N330-5h (Fig. 3e). The C1s spectra can be deconvoluted into sp2 C, sp3 C, C–O, and O–CO components located at ca. 284.5, 285.7, 286.5, and 289 eV, respectively (Fig. 3f, Fig. S3) [7]. The XPS results demonstrate that the sp3 C component is enhanced through air oxidation by the introduction of defects including pores and functional groups. And the oxygen-bearing groups primarily formed as C–O and O–CO are also increased (Fig. S3e), which can remarkably enhance the surface wettability of N330-5h towards aqueous electrolyte (as shown in Fig. S4) and provide additional pseudocapacitance. The first derivative XAES spectra of all the samples are shown in Fig. S5. The energy separation between the negative- and positive-going peaks represents the D value, which is 22.5 and 14.3 eV for graphite (100% sp2 structure) and diamond (100% sp3 structure), respectively [36–38]. The D values of all the samples are between 22.5 and 14.3 eV, demonstrating the sp2/sp3 hybridized structure of N330-x. Moreover, the D value decreases from 19.2 eV for N330 to 14.8 eV for N330-5h, further proving the gradually developed sp3 structure, which is in accordance with the XPS result.

Electric conductivity is one of the most important factors influencing the electrochemical performance of carbon materials. The conductivity of samples is tested by the I–V curves as shown in Fig. S6. The conductivity monotonically decreases from 194.8 S m−1 of original N330 to 57.3 S m−1 of N330-7h (Fig. 4 a). According to the XRD and Raman results, N330-1h exhibits enhanced crystallinity and lower defect density than N330, but still shows a decreased conductivity. This is because the introduction of micropores breaks the continuous conjugated structure and conductive networks, which is also proved by the low conductivity of microporous YP-50 activated carbon (39 S m−1).

The electrochemical performance of all the samples is measured in three-electrode system. All the CV curves of N330-x are distorted from rectangular shape with a pair of redox peak at ca. −0.6 V versus Hg/HgO reference electrode (Fig. 4b). And the galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles are also distorted from ideal isosceles triangle (Fig. 4c), indicating the combination of electrical double-layer behavior from high surface area and pseudocapacitive behavior of oxygen-bearing groups. The capacitance of N330 at 1 A g−1 is only 5 F g−1, and is greatly enhanced after air oxidation due to the improved surface area, abundant ion transport channels, and high oxygen content. N330-5h has the largest integral CV area and longest discharge time, leading to the highest capacitances of 175, 159, 152, 144 F g−1 at 1, 5, 10, and 50 A g−1, respectively, among all the samples. Importantly, N330-5h has outstanding rate capability with a high capacitance retention of 59% at 100 A g−1 (Fig. 4d), which is superior to most of the reported porous carbon materials (Table S1).

The EIS measurement is conducted to evaluate the electrochemical kinetics of the samples (Fig. 4e and f). The intersection to Z′ axis represents the electric resistance (Re), which increases with the oxidation time due to the reduced conductivity of N330-x. The semicircle diameter in high frequency region (Rct) gradually decreases after oxidation, indicating the improved charge transfer kinetics. What’s more, the slopes of the low-frequency tails of N330-x are higher than that of N330 and nearly vertical to the Z′ axis, proving the fast ion transport kinetics. The reason is due to the gradual evolution of mesopore-dominated hollow structure with high oxygen content in N330-x, leading to abundant electrolyte reservoirs, interconnected ion migration channels, as well as enhanced electrolyte wettability. The improved electrochemical kinetics by the novel structure is further proven by the Bode plots given in Fig. 4f. The specific capacitance increases 35.0 times from N330 to N330-5h. But the relaxation time constant (τ0) increases only 12.7 times from 0.067 s of N330 to 0.851 s of N330-5h, indicating the robust response ability of HCNPs.

To evaluate the application potential of the as-prepared HCNPs, we test the electrochemical performance of N330-5h//N330-5h symmetric devices with different mass loadings. The CV curves at 100 mV s−1 and the charge-discharge profiles at 1 A g−1 of the devices are almost overlapped (Fig. 5 a and b). And the areal capacitances of N330-5h//N330-5h are proportional to its mass loadings, achieving ultrahigh values of 2.31 and 1.59 F cm−2 at 1 and 10 A g−1, respectively, at the mass loading of 15 mg cm−2 (Fig. 5c). The areal capacitance and the rate performance are among the highest levels that have reported (Table S2). From the EIS curves, both the Rct and ion transport resistance of the device only show a slight increment with mass loading from 2 to 15 mg cm−2, proving the robust electrochemical kinetics (Fig. 5d). As shown in Fig. 5e, the inner cavities can store the electrolyte inside the thick electrode, and the pores on the conductive skins facilitate the ion transport. Therefore, the novel interconnected mesoporous hollow structure enables not only effective utilization of the active materials, but also fast charge-discharge ability for thick electrodes. Moreover, the symmetric device exhibits excellent cycle stability with no capacitance fading after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 5f), indicating the great potential for practical applications.

In conclusion, we prepared HCNPs from N330 carbon black through an extremely simple air oxidation method. During the oxidation process, the outer surface of N330 nanoparticles is firstly attacked by oxygen, and mesopores (3 nm) are formed and perforate through the skins. These pores further lead to the etching of the amorphous cores due to their high reactivity, forming large inner cavities (18 nm) and increasing the surface area from 87.4 to 753.2 m2 g−1. The HCNPs provide sufficient electrolyte storage spaces, and the interconnected mesopores on the conductive carbon skins facilitate ion migration kinetics, leading to outstanding capacitive and rate performance especially at high mass loadings. The assembled symmetric device with an ultrahigh mass loading of 15 mg cm−2 shows areal capacitance of 2.31 and 1.59 F cm−2 at 1 and 10 A g−1, respectively, indicating the practical application value of the as-prepared HCNPs.

Chengwei Fan: Writing - original draft. Yue Dong: Writing - original draft. Yifan Liu: Investigation. Lihui Zhang: Investigation. Dengke Wang: Methodology. Xieji Lin: Methodology. Yan Lv: Methodology. Su Zhang: Supervision. Huaihe Song: Supervision. Dianzeng Jia: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51702275, 21771157, U1610252), the Scientific Research Program of the Higher Education Institution of Xinjiang (XJEDU2017S003), Xinjiang Tianshan Xuesong Project (2018XS28), and Xinjiang Tianchi Doctoral Project.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards superior volumetric performance: design and preparation of novel carbon materials for energy storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhang, W. Lv, Y. Tao, Q.-H. Yang, Towards superior volumetric performance: design and preparation of novel carbon materials for energy storage, Energy Environ. Sci. 8 (2015) 1390-1403.
Y. Gogotsi, Energy storage wrapped up, Nature 509 (2014) 568.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient storage mechanisms for building better supercapacitors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boosting the electrical double-layer capacitance of graphene by self-doped defects through ball-milling

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of functionalized 3D hierarchical porous carbon for high-performance supercapacitors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ostwald ripening improves rate capability of high mass loading manganese oxide for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ban, K. Malek, C. Huang, Z. Liu, A molecular model for carbon black primary particles with internal nanoporosity, Carbon 49 (2011) 3362-3370.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon black filled conducting polymers and polymer blends

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Huang, Carbon black filled conducting polymers and polymer blends, Adv. Polym. Tech. 21 (2002) 299-313.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strongly coupled manganese ferrite/carbon black/polyaniline hybrid for low-cost supercapacitors with high rate capability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tang, J. Yang, X. Zhou, J. Xie, G. Chen, Oxidation of acetylene black by nitric acid in hermetically sealed condition, Microporous Mesoporous Mater. 193 (2014) 54-60.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The oxidation of graphitized carbon black

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.R. Smith, H. Polley, The Oxidation of Graphitized Carbon Black, J. Phys. Chem. 60 (1956) 689-691.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure and Properties of carbon black

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.D. Schaeffer, W.R. Smith, M.H. Polley, Structure and Properties of carbon black, Ind. Eng. Chem. 45 (1953) 1721-1725.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon onions produced by heat treatment of carbon soot and their relation to the 217.5 nm interstellar absorption feature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.A.D. Heer, D. Ugarte, Carbon onions produced by heat treatment of carbon soot and their relation to the 217.5 nm interstellar absorption feature, Chem. Phys. Lett. 207 (1993) 480-486.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The transformation of acetylene black into onion-like hollow carbon nanoparticles at 1000°C using an iron catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Lian, H. Song, X. Chen, L. Li, J. Huo, M. Zhao, et al., The transformation of acetylene black into onion-like hollow carbon nanoparticles at 1000°C using an iron catalyst, Carbon 46 (2008) 525-530.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidation rates of carbon black particles exposed to concentrated sunlight

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.H. Funken, E.L. Pfert, M. Hermes, K.B. Hne, B. Pohlmann, Oxidation rates of carbon black particles exposed to concentrated sunlight, Solar Energy 65 (1999) 15-19.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultramicroporous carbons puzzled by graphene quantum dots: integrated high gravimetric, volumetric, and areal capacitances for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, J. Zhu, Y. Qing, L. Wang, J. Zhao, J. Li, et al., Ultramicroporous Carbons Puzzled by Graphene Quantum Dots: Integrated High Gravimetric, Volumetric, and Areal Capacitances for Supercapacitors, Adv. Funct. Mater. 28 (2018) 1805898.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering cross-linking by coal-based graphene quantum dots toward tough, flexible, and hydrophobic electrospun carbon nanofiber fabrics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhu, S. Zhang, L. Wang, D. Jia, M. Xu, Z. Zhao, et al., Engineering cross-linking by coal-based graphene quantum dots toward tough, flexible, and hydrophobic electrospun carbon nanofiber fabrics, Carbon 129 (2018) 54-62.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boosting the supercapacitor performance of activated carbon by constructing overall conductive networks using graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Qing, Y. Jiang, H. Lin, L. Wang, A. Liu, Y. Cao, et al., Boosting the supercapacitor performance of activated carbon by constructing overall conductive networks using graphene quantum dots, J. Mater. Chem. A 7 (2019) 6021-6027.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Control of graphitization degree and defects of carbon blacks through ball-milling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, Y. Cui, B. Wu, R. Song, H. Song, J. Zhou, et al., Control of graphitization degree and defects of carbon blacks through ball-milling, RSC Adv. 4 (2014) 505-509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biomass-derived mesopore-dominant porous carbons with large specific surface area and high defect density as high performance electrode materials for Li-ion batteries and supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Niu, R. Shao, J. Liang, M. Dou, Z. Li, Y. Huang, et al., Biomass-derived mesopore-dominant porous carbons with large specific surface area and high defect density as high performance electrode materials for Li-ion batteries and supercapacitors, Nano Energy 36 (2017) 322-330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Breakdown of the adiabatic Born-Oppenheimer approximation in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Pisana, M. Lazzeri, C. Casiraghi, K.S. Novoselov, A.K. Geim, A.C. Ferrari, et al., Breakdown of the adiabatic Born-Oppenheimer approximation in graphene, Nat. Mater. 6 (2007) 198-201.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monitoring dopants by Raman scattering in an electrochemically top-gated graphene transistor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Das, S. Pisana, B. Chakraborty, S. Piscanec, S.K. Saha, U.V. Waghmare, et al., Monitoring dopants by Raman scattering in an electrochemically top-gated graphene transistor. Nat. Nanotechnol. 3 (2008) 210-215.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doping Dependence of the Raman spectrum of defected graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Bruna, A.K. Ott, M.I. S, D. Yoon, U. Sassi, A.C. Ferrari, Doping Dependence of the Raman spectrum of defected graphene, ACS Nano 8 (2014) 7432-7441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of the sp2/sp3 ratio in amorphous carbon structure by XPS and XAES

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Lascovich, R. Giorgi, S. Scaglione, Evaluation of the sp2/sp3 ratio in amorphous carbon structure by XPS and XAES, Appl. Surf. Sci. 47 (1991) 17-21.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison among XAES, PELS and XPS techniques for evaluation of sp2 percentage in a-C H

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Lascovich, S. Scaglione, Comparison among XAES, PELS and XPS techniques for evaluation of sp2 percentage in a-C H, Appl. Surf. Sci. 78 (1994) 17-23.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct evaluation of the sp3 content in diamond-like-carbon films by XPS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. MéRel, M. Tabbal, M. Chaker, S. Moisa, J. Margot, Direct evaluation of the sp3 content in diamond-like-carbon films by XPS, Appl. Surf. Sci. 136 (1998) 105-110.
